coredata.dc:title: Adsorption of Pyrimidin-2-amine (PA) on Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs): Non-covalent Interaction Study
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah, Zakir
Kim, Hyun Jee
Mary, Y. Sheena
Zhan, Xuan
Kwon, Hyung Wook
coredata.dc:description: A detailed theoretical study was implemented on a novel drug molecule “Pyrimidin-2-amine (PA)”. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) simulations were accomplished to explain the adsorption performance of the PA drug molecule on graphene quantum dots (GQD). Binding and adsorption energy, 4-energy molecular orbitals analysis, charge transfer effect, QTAIM, dipole moment, NLO properties, frontier molecular orbital analysis (FMO), the density of states (DOS), RDG-isosurfaces, UV–Visible, and SERS spectra analysis have been explored while performing the DFT simulations. The total adsorption energy of PA was calculated to be −3.45, –22.48, −20.32, −3.78, and −4.47 Kcal/mol for the corresponding CC-PA, BB-P-PA (where “P” stands for the para position of doped elements), NN-P-PA, BN-O-PA (where “O” stands for the ortho position of doped elements), and BN-P-PA complexes respectively. The intermolecular interaction forces were dogged by the establishment of numerous bond critical points (BCPs), monitored by ring critical points (RCPs) at the midpoint of the interaction state.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS
DFT
QTAIM
DOS
Non-covalent interaction
Adsorption
RGD iso-surfaces

Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science & Technology Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Priority Research Centers Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (202OR1A6A1A03041954), and Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science & Technology Development (Project No. PJ0147612022), and Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea and the Center for Women In Science, Engineering and Technology (WISET) Grant funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT) under the Program for Re- turners into R&D (Project No. WISET-2022-726).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PA molecule’s (a) optimized geometry (b) HOMO – LUMO plot (c) MEP plot.
Optimized geometries of (a) CC-PA (b) NN-P-PA (c) BB-P-PA (d) BN-O-PA (e) BN-P-PA.
MEP plots of (a) CC (b) BB-P (c) NN-P (d) BN-O (e) BN-P.
Calculated non-covalent interaction surfaces (left side) RDG-scatter graph (middle) and ESP plot of Complexes CC-PA (a), NN-P-PA (b), BB-P-PA (c), BN-O-PA (d), and BB-P-PA (e).
HOMO-LUMO plots of (a) CC-PA (b) BB-P-PA (c) NN-P-PA (d) BN-O-PA (e) BN-P-PA.
UV–Vis spectra of PA, CC-PA, BB-P-PA, NN-P-PA, BN-O-PA, and BN-P-PA.
DFT predicted Raman spectra of (a) PA drug and (b) PA-graphene quantum dot.
Single point Energies, dipole moment, and polarizability of PA, graphene quantum dots nano surfaces, and PA–Nano surfaces.
Non-linear optical properties of PA and PA-GQDs complexes.
Chemical descriptors (eV) of PA, graphene quantum dots Nano surfaces, and PA–Nano surfaces.

head.ce:title: Adsorption of Pyrimidin-2-amine (PA) on Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs): Non-covalent Interaction Study

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences and Bioengineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A detailed theoretical study was implemented on a novel drug molecule “Pyrimidin-2-amine (PA)”. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) simulations were accomplished to explain the adsorption performance of the PA drug molecule on graphene quantum dots (GQD). Binding and adsorption energy, 4-energy molecular orbitals analysis, charge transfer effect, QTAIM, dipole moment, NLO properties, frontier molecular orbital analysis (FMO), the density of states (DOS), RDG-isosurfaces, UV–Visible, and SERS spectra analysis have been explored while performing the DFT simulations. The total adsorption energy of PA was calculated to be −3.45, –22.48, −20.32, −3.78, and −4.47 Kcal/mol for the corresponding CC-PA, BB-P-PA (where “P” stands for the para position of doped elements), NN-P-PA, BN-O-PA (where “O” stands for the ortho position of doped elements), and BN-P-PA complexes respectively. The intermolecular interaction forces were dogged by the establishment of numerous bond critical points (BCPs), monitored by ring critical points (RCPs) at the midpoint of the interaction state.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, graphene and its derivatives have become the most investigated metal-free electro-catalytic materials [1]. Because metal catalysts’ widespread commercialization has been hampered by their expensive cost and corrosive instability, many derivatives have been described as electro-catalytic materials for degrading organic refractory pollutants [2]. Despite the fact that chemotherapy is one of the most extensively used cancer treatments most traditional chemotherapeutic drugs have substantial side effects throughout patient treatment [3]. As a result, various studies have been conducted to investigate effective drug delivery systems for delivering chemotherapeutic drugs selectively into tumor tissues [4,5]. Nanotechnology is a fascinating field of research with a wide range of applications. Different nanoscale drug carriers have recently been used for targeted drug delivery of malignancies [6]. Fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphene are nano-sized carbon allotropes that have emerged as intriguing prospects in nano-medicine, particularly as drug carriers [7]. Graphene quantum dots including doped ones have lately emerged as intriguing fluorescent probes with unique properties, such as broadband optical absorption, good chemical stability, high photo-bleaching stability, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, among others [8–11] (Fig. 1 ).

Due to their environmental impact and poor degrading performance in sewage treatment plants, pharmaceutical, and personal care items have provoked widespread concern as aquatic contaminants [12]. As a result, efficient treatment techniques are crucial in reducing the occurrence of pollutants in aquatic ecosystems. The electro-catalytic oxidation of metal-free materials, one of the most advanced oxidation methods available today, may efficiently avoid secondary contamination, emphasizing the necessity of electrode material selection. In the last decade, graphene and its derivatives have been the most investigated of these materials [13]. The electronic and chemical properties of carbon materials can be controlled using substitutional doping of nitrogen atoms, which may substitute for arbitrary carbon atoms, combine with vacancies in the lattice, and /or aggregate with other dopants, according to theoretical and experimental studies [14–17]. The N atom has been frequently employed for the chemical doping of a carbon nanomaterial due to its similar atomic size and five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms [18]. Doping graphene with substituent N atoms has the potential to alter its bandgap and provide novel features for device applications [19]. The heteroatom doping of boron, fluorine, phosphorus, nitrogen, and sulfur to improve the dispersion and electro-catalytic stability of graphene as an electro-catalytic material has received a lot of attention [20–24]. The B-doped graphene has a significant number of holes, which improves carrier concentration and thus graphene conductivity [25]. Boron, like nitrogen, is a viable dopant material because it has a lower electronegativity and hence generates p-type conductivity in graphene [26]. Several previous studies on the fabrication of B-doped graphene nanoparticles have been published [27–29]. Montejo-Alvaro et al. theoretically investigated the durability of Pd clusters supported by pristine, B-doped, and defective graphene quantum dots, as well as their reactivity toward oxygen adsorption [30]. According to Li et al. nitrogen-doped graphene emits a blue luminescence, which varies from its N-free counterpart’s green luminescence [31]. Juang et al reported the highly fluorescent green and red emissions from boron-doped graphene quantum dots under blue light illumination [32]. Recently a number of studies on the adsorption of drug molecules with graphene doped and undoped are reported [33–44].

Pyrimidine nucleus is a heterocyclic aromatic organic compound containing two nitrogen atoms at positions 1 and 3, like diazine of the six-membered ring which shows a wide range of biological activities [45]. Scaffolds containing the pyrimidine heterocycle exhibit various biological effects, including anti-inflammatory analgesic antimicrobial anticonvulsants, anti-tubercular anticancer, and antiviral activities [46–48]. In the literature survey, it has been described that compounds having a pyrimidine nucleus possess a broad range of biological activities. Pyrimidine and its bioisosteres are heterocyclic compounds with potent biological functions including anticancer and other activities.

Herein, we have presented PA drug adsorption on graphene quantum dots. For geometry optimization, the drug molecule was initially adjusted parallel to the graphene quantum dot at a distance of (less than3Å) which can permit maximum interaction of the drug molecule with the corresponding graphene quantum dots. Adsorption energy, charge transfer analysis, frontier molecular orbital analysis (FMO), QTAIM, the density of states (DOS), dipole moment, RDG-isosurfaces of non-covalent interaction, and UV–Visible, along with solvent effect has been considered while performing the theoretical study. The final complex stability was explored by adsorption energy.

All calculations were performed by using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) as implemented in the Gaussian 16 suit of ab initio suit suite of the program [49,50]. The visualization of the results was obtained by Gauss View 6.0.16 and GaussSum. The geometry optimization was further confirmed to be true minima by means of frequency calculation, showing no imaginary frequency. Adsorption of PA drug on the GQDs surfaces was calculated at the B3LYP-D3/6-311G* level of theory [51,52]. The binding energy, which is defined as a difference between the sum of the energies of the isolated molecules and their corresponding complexes, was used to assess the complexes’ stability. The density of state (DOS) results were obtained using the GaussSum program [53]. The electronic energies, (the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO), and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO)) were computed to show the change in their band gap. Also, some parameters related to HOMO and LUMO energies such as chemical hardness (h), chemical softness (S), electronegativity (χ), chemical potential (μ), and electrophilicity index (w) were computed by using the following formula:
(1)

The nonlinear optical (NLO) behaviors of molecular systems hinge on the polarization of electrons in their bonding orbitals. The mean polarization and anisotropy of the polarization α and Δα were premeditated as follows:

As we know first hyperpolarizability is a third-degree tensor that can be defined by a 3 × 3 × 3 matrix. The output of Gaussian 16 provides ten components of this matrix:
(6)

1 / 2

The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (AIM) has been widely smeared to inspect the real space functions and illustrate the nature of numerous inter and intramolecular interactions. The topology of PA-GQDs complexes was inspected according to Bader’s AIM theory, expending the Multiwfn code. Intermolecular interaction forces are eminent by the formation of numerous bond critical points (BCPs), trailed by ring critical points (RCPs) at the center of the interaction region. Analogously, BCPs between crystal-1 molecules and nitro-aromatic molecules are characterized by the values of electron density, ρ, and Laplacian of electron density, ∇2ρ. Reduced density gradient (RDG) has been used for developing gradient-corrected functions of increasing quality. With this tactic, mutually the nature and strength of the interactions are labeled in the iso-surface. Then, the RDG method is cast-off in this study for examining the strength and the interaction natures of complexes visually through the analysis of the simulated iso-surfaces.

Experiments have shown that N/B-doped graphene with various doping types and positions can exhibit a wide range of behaviors. As a result, four different graphene structures with N/B doping at the center are introduced in order to examine the function of doping type and position in the drug delivery system. In pristine nano-surfaces, the bond lengths are: C-C = 1.43 (CC), 1.42 to 1.47 (BB-P), 1.36 to 1.46 (NN-P), 1.43 (BN-O), 1.37 to 1.42 (BN-P); C-B = 1.53 (BB-P), 1.49 (BN-O and BN-P); C-N = 1.41 (NN-P), 1.41 to 1.43 Å (BN-O and BN-P).

To achieve maximum interaction, the PA molecule is first put parallel to the nano-surfaces at a separation of less than 3 Å, and then comprehensive geometric optimization is performed. The PA molecule assumes an edge on adsorption with CC and BN-O nano-surfaces through the O19-H20 and O5-H6 groups with a separation of 2.33, 2.59, and 2.58, 3.06 from the nano-surfaces, respectively (Figs. 2 and 3 ). For the other systems, there is a perpendicular orientation for the PA molecule with respect to the nano-surfaces and the interaction occurs through O7-H8 and NH2 groups. The separation between PA molecules are the nano-surfaces are 2.90 (OH) and 3.33 (NH2) for BB-P-PA, 2.54 (OH), and 3.55 (NH2) for NN-P-PA and 2.39 (OH) and 2.81 (NH2) for BN-P-PA systems. After PA adsorption on nano-surfaces, there is no change for C-C length in CC nano-surface while the C-C length becomes 1.41 to 1.43 in BB-P-PA, 1.38 to 1.43 in NN-P-PA, 1.42 to 1.44 in BN-O-PA and 1.38 to 1.43 in BN-P-PA systems. The C-N bond lengths become 1.41 to 1.44 in NN-P-PA, 1.40 to 1.42 in BN-O-PA and 1.41 to 1.43 in BN-P-PA and the B-C bond lengths become 1.55 in BB-P-PA, 1.48 to 1.50in BN-O-PA and 1.49 to 1.50 in BN–P–PA systems.

The binding locations have been investigated in order to have a better understanding of them by means of molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps. The MEPs of pristine and N/B-doped graphene are shown in Fig. 3 for this purpose. The blue areas have a low concentration of electrons whereas the red parts have a high concentration of electrons. In PA the most reactive sites are oxygen (red and yellow) and hydrogen (blue) (Fig. 3). The middle rings of CC and BB-P formations have an abundance of electrons, whereas the MEP in the B atom containing BN-O and BN-P systems are electron-rich sites (Fig. 3). For NN-P the center part is blue having a low concentration of electrons and the outer parts of the system are yellow giving electron-rich regions [54,54]. As seen in Fig. 2 the adsorption distances of PA drug from the nano-carriers indicate the non-covalent nature of interactions [55]. As seen in Fig. 2, after PA adsorption on the nano-surfaces, there is redistribution for the electron-rich and deficient regions which shows the charge transfer in the systems.

By computing the binding energies (ΔEb) between the PA drug and nano-carrier surfaces, the stability of the drug-carrier complexes was studied. As it is seen from Table 1, the magnitudes of the binding energy values follow the order BB-P-PA (–23.27) > NN-P-PA (-20.87) > BN-P-PA (-4.83) > BN-O-PA (-4.27) > CC-PA (-3.87). The magnitude of ΔEb between the PA drug and the center identical atoms doped in parallel positions in the graphene is greater than that between other systems, as seen by this sequence.

The thermodynamic parameters are calculated and reported in Table 2 to understand the viability of the PA drug adsorption process on nano-carrier surfaces. The order of ΔH values for the PA adsorption on the nano-carrier surfaces is as follows: BB-P-PA (-21.60) > NN-P-PA (-19.47) > BN-P-PA (-4.73) > BN-O-PA (-2.89) > CC-PA (-2.56) and a similar order is seen also for the ΔS values. The ΔG values are negative for BB-P/NN-P-PA systems indicating that the PA drug adsorption on nano-surfaces occurs spontaneously while for other systems, all the values are less and positive. The ΔGad values for PA drug adsorption on the identical substitution at para positions on the center of the nano-system, on the other hand, are higher than those for the pristine and other systems. The negative value of ΔHad also implies that PA drug adsorption on nano-carrier surfaces is exothermic [56].

Fig. 4, portrays together the AIM, RDG, and ESP analysis of the CC-PA (a), NN-P-PA (b), BB-P-PA (c), BN-O-PA (d), and BB-P-PA (e) complexes. Mathematical data for simulated BCPs schemes were achieved according to Bader's theory, ρ and ∇2ρ for all studied DFT cases. According to AIM theory H-bonding should have ρ and ∇2ρ in the range of 0.002–0.04 a.u. and 0.020 to 0.139 a.u, congruently. The BCPs for every complex interaction are measured for front positions, it was observed that the BN-O-PA complex display bond critical points(BCP) with the highest values of ρ and ∇2ρ for all the complexes, though, in the case of BB-P-PA and NN-P-PA the later shows strong interactions as compared to the earlier counterpart complex. In the reduced density gradient (RDG) the dissimilar colors of the interaction surface demonstrate the magnitude of the interaction and repulsion. The blue color attitudes for strong interaction and the green or green–brown color spectacles the moderate type of interaction, while the red color presents repulsive forces or ring strain.

Table 1 shows the dipole moment estimations before and after the PA drug adsorption on the nanocarrier surfaces. As exhibited in Table 1, the CC, BB-P, and NN-P structures are nonpolar (zero dipole moments), while BN-O and BN-P structures are polar with dipole moments of 4.5607 and 4.6482 Debye respectively. The dipole moment values increase after the PA drug adsorbs on the nano-carrier surfaces except for the BN-O surface, implying that the nanocarrier-PA complexes are soluble in an aqueous media. The polarizability of all PA-nanocarriers is higher than that of pristine nano-surfaces except for CC nano-surface and much greater than that of PA molecule showing the charge transfer in the complexes with higher nonlinear optical activity.

The energies of FMOs and the bandgap for nano-carriers, PA drug, and their complexes are computed to better understand the influence of the nano-carrier, the PA drug adsorption on the electronic properties of the nano-carrier (Table 2). The graphene quantum dot’s HOMO-LUMO energy gap was found to be 4.0833 eV which is consistent with prior findings (4.32 eV) [61]. Table 2 displays that all N/B doped graphene structures have small HOMO-LUMO energy gaps in comparison with that of graphene quantum dots’s so that CC (4.0833) > BN-O (2.8155) > BN-P (2.7826) > NN-P (0.3442) > BB-P (0.3064) and similar B or N atoms doping in the para position of the graphene quantum dot’s ring show minimum energy gap. Adsorption of PA drug on the nano-carrier surfaces results in a change in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap values: a large change is observed for BB/NN-P while (increase), whereas adsorption of PA drug on dissimilar doping results in only a small change in the energy gap. Upon adsorption of the PA molecule, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels are also altered.

The TD-DFT gives two adsorptions at 210.39 and 239.27 and after adsorption, the wavelength moves to the middle of the visible region for all nanocarrier-PA molecule complexes. For NN-P-PA the adsorption is maximum at 673.13 while CC-PA gives a minimum value of 274.81 nm. For dissimilar atom doping complexes, a doublet is found in the UV–Vis absorption (Fig. 5). The DFT predicted Raman spectra of PA molecule before and after adsorption is given in Fig. 6. A comparison of the Intensity reveals that most of the vibrational modes of PA molecule are enhanced after the adsorption process due to surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). Also, the inactive Raman modes of PA molecule are active in the adsorption Raman spectrum with enhancement in intensity. Especially NH2, OH, and ring modes possess more Raman intensity after adsorption. Maximum enhancement is observed for NN-P and BB-P which support the high adsorption energy of these complexes.

We have accomplished a comprehensive theoretical study of a novel PA drug molecule adsorption and non-covalent interactions study on graphene quantum dots surface, using DFT and TD-DFT simulations along with B3LYP-D3 level of theory with 6-311G**(d, p) basis set. Various constraints, RDG scattered graph, NLO properties, RDG iso-surfaces, etc. were employed to identify the sensation of PA adsorption on graphene quantum dots’ surfaces. Among the studied cases, the BN–P–PA complex was found to be the utmost firm complex with a ΔG value of −13.38 kcal/more, ΔE of –23.49, and ΔH of 22.60 kcal/mol.

Zakir Ullah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft, Validation. Jyun Jee Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Y. Sheena Mary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Xuan Zhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Hyung Wook Kwon: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Priority Research Centers Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (202OR1A6A1A03041954), and Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science & Technology Development (Project No. PJ0147612022), and Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea and the Center for Women In Science, Engineering and Technology (WISET) Grant funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT(MSIT) under the Program for Re- turners into R&D (Project No. WISET-2022-726).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.120654.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrapure graphene is a poor electrocatalytic: definitive proof of the key role of metallic impurities in graphene-based electrocatalysis

Chem. Int. Ed. 55 (2016) 11849-11853, https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201605961

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomedicine: an effective tool in cancer therapy
